title: Homer And Lisa Exchange Cross Words

jokes.text: Buy a cup, or I punch my sister!
jokes.setup: Bart is trying to sell lemonade with typical marketing tactics
jokes.punchline: Threatens violence against his sister as a sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart - turning family dysfunction into a business model. Great character integration and economy.

jokes.text: It's true! He's not bluffing!
jokes.setup: Bart threatens to punch Lisa to sell lemonade
jokes.punchline: Lisa confirms the threat is real, becoming complicit in her own endangerment
jokes.explanation: Lisa's earnest confirmation of Bart's threat shows their dysfunctional sibling dynamic perfectly.

jokes.text: What are we, a piano bar?
jokes.setup: Suggestion to put money in the jar to prime the pump
jokes.punchline: Lisa's comparison to tipping culture at piano bars
jokes.explanation: Smart comparison that shows Lisa's precocious understanding of adult social conventions.

jokes.text: Lousy dog. If you had your way, I'd walk you every day.
jokes.setup: Homer buying multiple lemonades
jokes.punchline: His complaint about his dog wanting daily walks as if it's unreasonable
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer - treating basic pet care as an unreasonable demand.

jokes.text: Keep moving, deadbeat. Cash only.
jokes.setup: Customer wants to put lemonade on his tab
jokes.punchline: Bart runs his stand like a hardened business owner dealing with bad credit risks
jokes.explanation: Bart channeling adult business cynicism through a lemonade stand is classic Simpsons.

jokes.text: Fine. Lemonade's for babies anyways. I'm going to Moe's, for beer!
jokes.setup: Barney can't get lemonade on credit
jokes.punchline: Dismisses lemonade as childish while going to a bar instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barney - his priorities and logic perfectly backwards.

jokes.text: Lisa, can Daddy borrow your bike?
jokes.setup: Homer needs transportation
jokes.punchline: Adult man asking to borrow his daughter's bicycle, implying he'll look ridiculous riding it
jokes.explanation: Simple visual comedy of Homer's dignity being flexible when convenient.

jokes.text: I understand. It can be very difficult for a woman to propose marriage.
jokes.setup: Edna says she has something difficult to say
jokes.punchline: Skinner completely misreads the situation as a marriage proposal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner obliviousness - his romantic delusions override obvious social cues.

jokes.text: But I am willing to go halvsies on a ring.
jokes.setup: Skinner thinks Edna is proposing
jokes.punchline: His cheap offer to split the cost of an engagement ring
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Skinner's cheapness even in romantic moments - character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: Any of you lugs want to break up with my boyfriend for me? I'll buy you a beer.
jokes.setup: Edna can't break up with Skinner directly
jokes.punchline: She outsources the breakup to bar patrons for the price of a beer
jokes.explanation: Absurd but believable solution that sets up Homer's new career path. Great premise establishment.

jokes.text: No kids tying you down or a crippling mortgage that you refinanced at 26% because a dancing Internet cowboy told you to.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to cheer up Skinner about being single
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific financial mistake involving a dancing internet cowboy
jokes.explanation: The dancing internet cowboy is brilliantly specific and perfectly captures early 2000s predatory lending with Homer-level gullibility.

jokes.text: Gee, compared to you, my life is one big half-day.
jokes.setup: Homer's life complaints make Skinner feel better
jokes.punchline: Skinner's awkward attempt at the phrase 'holiday' shows his social awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Skinner's mangled idiom perfectly shows his stunted social development.

jokes.text: and they were state runners up in 1997.
jokes.setup: Skinner says Homer cheered him up better than the pep squad
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific and modest credential about the pep squad
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner detail - he remembers and takes pride in very minor school achievements.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm afraid Lenny's dead. What? I just talked to him. And you will again, because he isn't really dead.
jokes.setup: Doreen asks if Lenny is here
jokes.punchline: Homer's unnecessarily dramatic fake death announcement followed by immediate retraction
jokes.explanation: Homer's bizarrely convoluted breakup strategy shows his unique problem-solving approach.

jokes.text: Great gig. It is a great gig.
jokes.setup: Inspector mentions his part-time field agent job
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate enthusiastic agreement creates awkward moment
jokes.explanation: Simple awkward beat showing Homer's social cluelessness.

jokes.text: 35 cents? Where did that come from? Can't be mine. Must be yours. I don't care if it's 45 cents, I don't want it.
jokes.setup: Money appears during the licensing discussion
jokes.punchline: Bart's attempt to disown the money while Lisa inflates the amount
jokes.explanation: Shows the kids' panic at authority but the execution is a bit weak.

jokes.text: Geez, I'm thirsty. You got any lemonade? Not anymore!
jokes.setup: Inspector just shut down the lemonade stand
jokes.punchline: He immediately asks for lemonade, getting an indignant response
jokes.explanation: Good ironic timing - authority figure creates the problem he then faces.

jokes.text: Come on, come on. I was halfway through an operation when my license expired.
jokes.setup: People waiting for licenses
jokes.punchline: Doctor admits to operating without valid license
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy about bureaucratic priorities, economical and shocking

jokes.text: Excitement. Oh, wait, that don't fit.
jokes.setup: Clerk trying to solve 'Franklin Roosevelt's middle name'
jokes.punchline: His completely wrong guess and casual grammar
jokes.explanation: Shows the clerk's incompetence while establishing Lisa's puzzle expertise.

jokes.text: I got it! 'Isaac Bashevis!'
jokes.setup: Clue is 'Singer of Yentl' for 13 letters
jokes.punchline: Clerk confidently gives wrong answer (Isaac Bashevis Singer was a writer, not the singer/actress Barbra Streisand)
jokes.explanation: Shows clerk's confident incompetence - confusing the famous writer with Barbra Streisand.

jokes.text: Whoo, what is a four-letter word for 'hero'? 'Hero.' Yes, but I meant 'Lisa.'
jokes.setup: Homer asks for four-letter word for hero
jokes.punchline: After getting the obvious answer, reveals he meant his daughter's name
jokes.explanation: Sweet father-daughter moment disguised as a puzzle clue. Great misdirection and character warmth.

jokes.text: The gay guy? That's not all I am! Well, it's a lot of what I am.
jokes.setup: Homer identifies Grady as 'the gay guy'
jokes.punchline: Grady's offended protest followed by immediate honest admission
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Grady's self-awareness and the contradiction between offense and honesty.

jokes.text: Marge, I'm going to a hardcore gay club, and I won't be home till 3:00 in the morning! Have fun!
jokes.setup: Homer announces his evening plans to Marge
jokes.punchline: Marge's casual, supportive response to what would normally be concerning news
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge reaction - her unflappable acceptance of Homer's weirdness after years of marriage.

jokes.text: You've gone completely fruit loops. 'Fruit Loops.' That's the answer to 38 down: 'Toucan's delight.'
jokes.setup: Bart says Lisa has gone fruit loops
jokes.punchline: Lisa immediately connects it to a crossword clue
jokes.explanation: Establishes Lisa's crossword obsession perfectly - she can't hear normal conversation without seeing puzzles.

jokes.text: No, I mean you're loco in the co. 'Cocoa': 'Ice rink drink.'
jokes.setup: Bart tries another insult about Lisa's mental state
jokes.punchline: Lisa again turns his words into crossword answers
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the running gag - Lisa's obsession is driving Bart crazy.

jokes.text: 'Bart': 'San Francisco people mover.'
jokes.setup: Lisa continues turning conversation into crossword clues
jokes.punchline: She turns Bart's name into a clue about BART transit system
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay using Bart's name as both character and Bay Area Rapid Transit - shows Lisa's puzzle brain working.

jokes.text: In his what?
jokes.setup: Homer tells Julio he'll always have a special place in Grady's heart
jokes.punchline: Julio's confused/hurt reaction to Homer's breakup cliche
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat showing the inadequacy of Homer's breakup technique.

jokes.text: Another religion? You know, you're just gonna drop the whole thing when you go to college and get a Jewish boyfriend.
jokes.setup: Lisa announces she's a cruciverbalist
jokes.punchline: Marge's assumption it's another religion and her specific prediction about Lisa's future
jokes.explanation: Great Marge moment - her weary familiarity with Lisa's phases and oddly specific future prediction.

jokes.text: Back then, we called them Alphabet Hotels, 'cause every letter gets its own little room.
jokes.setup: Grandpa says he's done crosswords since 1958
jokes.punchline: His folksy, completely made-up old-timey name for crosswords
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa invention - sounds plausible as old slang while being completely absurd. Excellent character voice.

jokes.text: 'Will' and 'shorts'-- two things I'm no longer allowed to change by myself.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions Will Shortz
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's dark admission about his loss of independence
jokes.explanation: Dark Grandpa humor about aging and loss of dignity, perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Who's driving the bus? What bus?
jokes.setup: Two characters are so absorbed in crosswords they're oblivious to surroundings
jokes.punchline: They're apparently on a driverless bus but don't notice
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing how crosswords can consume people's attention.

jokes.text: Sweet Conclusions Breakup Service... thanks to our patented Tender Dump System.
jokes.setup: Homer's professional breakup service
jokes.punchline: The business name and 'Tender Dump System' - mixing gentle language with harsh reality
jokes.explanation: Clever business naming that captures Homer's attempt at professionalism while being inherently crude.

jokes.text: What in the blue blazes are you doing to my hopscotching grids?
jokes.setup: Willie discovers Lisa has altered the playground markings
jokes.punchline: Willie's term 'hopscotching grids' instead of hopscotch courts
jokes.explanation: Willie's unique vocabulary and outrage at his grounds being altered is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You called? Made reference. My mistake.
jokes.setup: Willie mentions dealing with Skinner
jokes.punchline: Skinner appears thinking he was called, then Willie clarifies
jokes.explanation: Good running gag showing Skinner's eagerness to be involved and Willie's exasperation.

jokes.text: You called? You misheard. My mistake.
jokes.setup: Willie mentions his pool boy using his skimmer
jokes.punchline: Same gag as Skinner but with different character
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that escalates the running gag but not as strong as the original.

jokes.text: We are the spirits of the relationships you killed.
jokes.setup: Homer has a nightmare about his breakup business
jokes.punchline: Supernatural consequences for his actions presented dramatically
jokes.explanation: Good dream sequence setup for Homer's conscience catching up with him.

jokes.text: We're the babies those couples would have had.
jokes.setup: Spirits of broken relationships haunt Homer
jokes.punchline: Ghost babies representing potential children from broken couples
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny escalation of the consequences - very Simpsons blend of absurd and profound.

jokes.text: And we're the antiques those couples would have bought together.
jokes.setup: Escalating supernatural consequences
jokes.punchline: Even future furniture purchases are mourning the broken relationships
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd escalation - from babies to furniture is perfectly Simpsons surreal logic.

jokes.text: Surely some holds are barred. No, none.
jokes.setup: Official describes crossword tournament as 'no-holds-barred'
jokes.punchline: Lisa's logical question met with literal confirmation
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment showing her literal mind questioning dramatic language.

jokes.text: And parlay with the Asian kid. He has a name, you know. What is it? I don't know. He's not my kid.
jokes.setup: Homer wants to bet on Lisa and the Asian kid
jokes.punchline: Moe calls out the casual racism but admits he doesn't know the kid's name either
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing casual prejudice getting called out then revealed as mutual ignorance.

jokes.text: Just be yourself. I want to change my bet. I'm going against my daughter.
jokes.setup: Homer encourages Lisa to be herself
jokes.punchline: Immediately bets against her based on her self-doubt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - treating his daughter's psychological insight as a betting tip.

jokes.text: I'll take your money, but I won't look you in the eye.
jokes.setup: Homer bets against his own daughter
jokes.punchline: Moe's moral compromise with conditions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe - greedy but retaining some vestige of shame about enabling Homer's betrayal.

jokes.text: You know, my wife Shirley, she's the real crossword ace in our family. Fills in those letters fast as hail on a tin roof: ping-ping-ping, she's done.
jokes.setup: Elderly competitor talks about his wife's crossword skills
jokes.punchline: His folksy description suggests he's just a harmless old man
jokes.explanation: Sets up the hustler reveal with genuine-seeming folksy charm.

jokes.text: Hey, what can you do? Life keeps changing the music, but you gotta keep up the cha-cha-cha, huh?
jokes.setup: Old man seems sad about his deceased wife
jokes.punchline: His oddly upbeat philosophy about adapting to life changes
jokes.explanation: Continues building the character before the reveal, mixing genuine sentiment with suspicious cheerfulness.

jokes.text: Hey, there are no lenses in these glasses.
jokes.setup: Old man drops his glasses during the competition
jokes.punchline: Lisa discovers they're fake, revealing he's been hustling
jokes.explanation: Good reveal of the hustler setup - fake glasses are a classic con artist prop.

jokes.text: Let's see, that's 'Enrico Fermi', and there you've got the 'Pet Shop Boys,' and cross the T and zing the Z and skit-skat skoodily I'm all the way home.
jokes.setup: Old man reveals his true crossword skills
jokes.punchline: His rapid-fire solving with made-up enthusiastic sound effects
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the hustler setup with colorful made-up crossword slang.

jokes.text: Here's your money... drenched in your daughter's tears.
jokes.setup: Homer collects his winnings after betting against Lisa
jokes.punchline: Moe's dramatic description of the tainted money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe line mixing his poetic tendencies with moral judgment of Homer's betrayal.

jokes.text: You know, for a bartender-bookie, you're awfully judgmental.
jokes.setup: Moe judges Homer for betting against Lisa
jokes.punchline: Homer points out the hypocrisy of moral judgment from someone in illegal gambling
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - deflecting guilt by pointing out others' moral compromises.

jokes.text: He bought new shoelaces with fancy metal tips.
jokes.setup: Family notices Homer seems different
jokes.punchline: Lisa's keen observation of minor luxury purchases
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment - her attention to detail revealing Homer's newfound wealth.

jokes.text: What's that, Mr. Teddy? You're saying that you know women, and that when women say they're not mad, they're madder than ever?
jokes.setup: Homer uses his teddy bear to interpret Lisa's anger
jokes.punchline: Mr. Teddy's supposed wisdom about women's communication
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment using his teddy bear as relationship advisor shows his emotional immaturity.

jokes.text: From now on, I'm Lisa Bouvier!
jokes.setup: Lisa disowns Homer as her father
jokes.punchline: She takes her mother's maiden name as ultimate rejection
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Lisa's anger with a formal name change showing her seriousness.

jokes.text: So the name 'Lisa Simpson' is available, huh? Lotta goodwill attached to that name. I gonna take it. Lisa's Tavern, Lisa speaking.
jokes.setup: Lisa has abandoned the Simpson name
jokes.punchline: Moe immediately tries to capitalize on the available name for his business
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Moe moment - his opportunistic business instincts and complete lack of sentiment about family drama.

jokes.text: Back in high school, the boys used to call me Marge 'Boobier.'
jokes.setup: Marge tries to comfort Lisa about taking her maiden name
jokes.punchline: Her embarrassing high school nickname based on her last name
jokes.explanation: Good Marge moment revealing embarrassing personal history while trying to be supportive.

jokes.text: What did you think her name was before it was Kennedy? Jackie 'O.' Like SpaghettiOs. I thought that was where her money came from.
jokes.setup: Homer learns Jackie Kennedy's maiden name was Bouvier
jokes.punchline: His belief that Jackie O's wealth came from SpaghettiOs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - connecting Jackie O to SpaghettiOs fortune is brilliantly stupid and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: My innocence died in those 441 little boxes.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Lisa do crosswords to cheer up
jokes.punchline: Lisa's dramatic description of losing her crossword innocence
jokes.explanation: Great Lisa line - her tendency toward dramatic literary language applied to crossword trauma.

jokes.text: Diets! Oh, Will Shortz, you clever rascal.
jokes.setup: Lisa solves the last crossword clue: 'Loses on purpose'
jokes.punchline: Her appreciation for the puzzle constructor's wordplay
jokes.explanation: Shows Lisa's genuine appreciation for wordplay craftsmanship and sets up the message reveal.

jokes.text: Diets! Oh, Will Shortz, you clever rascal.
jokes.setup: Lisa solving crossword clue 'Loses on purpose'
jokes.punchline: Appreciation for puzzle editor's cleverness
jokes.explanation: Shows Lisa's crossword enthusiasm, sets up the planted message reveal

jokes.text: 'Dumb Dad Sorry For His Bet.' Wow, it's almost as if Dad planted that message. No, it must just be a weird coincidence. But what if it's not? Oh, it must be. Or mustn't it not?
jokes.setup: Lisa finds suspicious crossword message
jokes.punchline: Her internal logical debate with confused conclusion
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's analytical nature getting confused

jokes.text: New York Times Crossword Editor Will Shortz! And master puzzle constructor Merl Reagle!
jokes.setup: Homer reveals he had help creating the crossword message
jokes.punchline: He enlisted real-world crossword celebrities
jokes.explanation: Good meta joke using real crossword world celebrities in Homer's grand gesture.

jokes.text: And I edited it. Now get back to crosswording!
jokes.setup: Will Shortz appears as himself
jokes.punchline: His stern command to return to crossword puzzles
jokes.explanation: Decent cameo moment playing on Shortz's authority in the crossword world.
